agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have presented directly contradictory requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency as a competitive differentiator, while Operations demands no location sharing due to driver retention concerns. This is a fundamental business conflict that cannot be resolved at the engineering level — it requires executive alignment on company strategy.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Courier Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A driver or delivery person who physically transports a package from origin to destination."},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates updated at frequent intervals during an active delivery."},{"term":"Delivery window","definition":"The time period during which a courier is actively en route to deliver a specific package to a specific customer."},{"term":"Location granularity","definition":"The precision level at which a courier's position is exposed — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to zone-level approximation (e.g., '2 stops away')."},{"term":"Active delivery","definition":"The state in which a courier has picked up a package and is in transit to the customer's delivery address."},{"term":"Driver retention","definition":"The operational metric tracking courier workforce stability; attrition directly impacts delivery capacity and cost."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (e.g., traffic, break) and customer sees no movement","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — depends on whether location is shared at all, and at what granularity. Exact GPS makes this highly visible and stressful for couriers. Zone-level abstraction masks it."},{"scenario":"Courier deviates from expected route (multi-stop optimization)","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — exact GPS would show the courier moving away from the customer, generating support tickets. Abstracted progress indicators would not."},{"scenario":"Courier is near customer's home but delivering to a neighbor first","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — exact GPS reveals the courier is on the customer's street but not stopping, creating confusion and complaints."},{"scenario":"Off-duty courier whose device still transmits location","expected_behavior":"Regardless of resolution, location must ONLY be available during an active delivery. No tracking outside delivery windows. This is likely a legal requirement under labor/privacy law."},{"scenario":"Customer screenshots or records courier location data","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — higher granularity increases privacy and safety risk to couriers. This is a factor in the resolution decision."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High impact either way. Customer Success argues visibility drives conversion and retention (revenue). Operations argues driver attrition drives recruitment and training costs ($3K-$7K per driver in typical courier operations). Both are correct — the question is which cost dominates, and whether a middle path exists.","operational":"Driver retention directly constrains delivery capacity. Losing experienced couriers degrades on-time delivery rates, increases failed deliveries, and raises per-delivery cost. However, lack of tracking visibility increases inbound support volume ('where is my package?') which also has operational cost.","user":"Customers want delivery predictability — they want to know WHEN their package arrives so they can plan. Whether they need EXACT GPS or simply accurate ETA and progress updates is an unvalidated assumption. Couriers want to work without feeling surveilled — this is a workforce dignity concern that directly affects job satisfaction.","risk":"LEGAL/PRIVACY RISK: Sharing exact courier GPS coordinates may violate labor privacy regulations in multiple jurisdictions (GDPR, state-level privacy laws). This has not been addressed by either team and represents a potential compliance liability. SAFETY RISK: Exact courier locations could be exploited by bad actors. RETENTION RISK: Operations has reported measurable driver attrition already occurring.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Want delivery predictability. Exact GPS is one solution but not the only one. ETA-based or zone-based tracking may satisfy the underlying need."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected by surveillance concern. Workforce has already signaled attrition. Their experience determines operational capacity."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Owns customer satisfaction metrics. Believes transparency is a differentiator but may be conflating 'transparency' with 'exact GPS coordinates.'"},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Owns driver workforce. Experiencing measurable retention problem they attribute to location tracking."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED. Sharing real-time worker GPS with third parties has significant privacy and labor law implications."},{"group":"Executive leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. The decision encodes a strategic priority: customer experience vs. workforce stability. Engineering cannot make this call."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the spec contains a direct contradiction between two non-negotiable stakeholder requirements. No acceptance criteria can be defined until this is resolved. Additionally, a critical stakeholder (Legal/Compliance) has not been consulted on what may be a regulated decision. The two teams may both be presenting maximalist positions when a middle-ground solution (zone-level tracking, ETA-based updates) could satisfy both — but that compromise has not been validated with either party.","high_confidence_areas":["Location data must only be available during active deliveries — never outside delivery windows","Legal/privacy review is required before any courier location feature ships","The underlying customer need is delivery predictability, not necessarily exact GPS","Driver retention is a real operational constraint that cannot be dismissed"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether exact GPS, zone-level, or ETA-based tracking is the correct granularity","Whether customers actually need or want exact coordinates vs. accurate ETAs","Whether Operations' attrition claim is specifically caused by location tracking or correlated with other factors","Jurisdictional privacy requirements for sharing worker location with customers"],"assumptions":["Both teams are presenting genuine business concerns, not political positions","Driver attrition is at least partially attributable to location visibility concerns","Customer satisfaction with delivery can be achieved through multiple tracking granularity levels","Legal and compliance review has not yet occurred"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers as non-negotiable. Operations requires never showing courier location to customers as non-negotiable. These are mutually exclusive requirements from peer-level stakeholders. Engineering cannot resolve this — it requires executive decision on strategic priority, informed by: (1) actual customer research on what tracking granularity satisfies their need, (2) actual driver retention data proving causation not just correlation, and (3) legal review of privacy obligations.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers/Drivers","Customer Success","Operations","Legal/Compliance","Executive Leadership"],"options":["A: Show exact GPS coordinates (satisfies Customer Success, violates Operations requirement, potential legal risk)","B: Show no location at all (satisfies Operations, violates Customer Success requirement)","C: Show abstracted location — zone-level or progress-based ('3 stops away', 'Arriving in ~10 minutes') — potential compromise that gives customers predictability without exact surveillance of couriers","D: Courier opt-in model — let couriers choose whether to share location, accept inconsistent customer experience"],"recommendation":"OPTION C — Zone-level or ETA-based tracking. This is the standard industry solution (UberEats, DoorDash, Amazon all use abstracted representations, not raw GPS). It satisfies the customer's actual need (predictability) while protecting courier privacy. However, this recommendation CANNOT be implemented until: (1) Executive leadership arbitrates the conflict, (2) Legal reviews privacy obligations, (3) Customer research validates that abstracted tracking meets satisfaction goals, and (4) Driver feedback confirms the approach addresses retention concerns. DO NOT BUILD ANYTHING until this is resolved."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot accept either requirement as stated — they are mutually exclusive","business_rule_broken":"No coherent business rule can encode both 'always show exact GPS' and 'never show location'"},{"violation":"Legal/Compliance stakeholder not consulted","business_rule_broken":"Sharing real-time worker location with third parties is a regulated activity in many jurisdictions. No feature spec should proceed without legal review."},{"violation":"Customer need assumed without validation","business_rule_broken":"Customer Success equates 'transparency' with 'exact GPS coordinates' — this assumption has not been validated with actual customer research. The underlying need may be satisfied by less invasive means."}]}
